digest.top_line: Single-fraction lung SABR (pooled n=1687): 2-yr LC 90-93%, G3+ AEs 2-3% · one-and-done SABR holds up across primary NSCLC + pulmonary oligomets.
digest.tldr: Three large observational RT series, all pointing toward de-escalation: single-fraction lung SABR (n=1687) matches multi-fraction LC, TMT preserves bladder in MIBC (CLR 63.7%, n=369), and PSMA-PET (NPV ~96%) may spare ePLND. Thoracic carries the day; none randomised.

sites.intro: Retrospective multicenter TMT bladder-preservation cohort; no randomised TMT-vs-cystectomy comparator, RT dose/fractionation unreported.

studies.name: Multicenter TMT Bladder Preservation Analysis (n=369)
studies.tldr: CLR 63.7% in 369 MIBC pts on definitive TMT; 5-FU-based CRT and better image guidance predicted higher complete local response.
details: 📊 1° endpoint: complete local response (CLR) 63.7% with definitive TMT (TURBT + concurrent chemoRT)
📊 Salvage cystectomy 9.7%
📊 Multivariable predictors of CLR
🔍 Multicenter retrospective cohort, Spain 2010-2022; N=369, median age 76, 85.1% male; cT2-T4aN0M0
🔍 RT dose/fractionation and modality mix not reported in source
⚠️ Retrospective; CLR predictors exploratory, no randomised TMT-vs-cystectomy comparator
⚠️ Weekly-portal-imaging signal likely confounded by treatment era/center technique, not a clean IGRT effect
🔗 Authors claim survival comparable to international TMT series (e.g. RTOG pooled, BC2001); no comparative numbers in source

verdict.rationale: Retrospective real-world European cohort; no randomised TMT-vs-cystectomy comparator. Reinforces guideline-recognized bladder preservation; predictor findings exploratory.

studies.significance: The two modifiable levers here are RT delivery and chemo backbone, both in the radonc's hands: 5-FU-based CRT predicted higher CLR (OR 4.9) and weekly portal imaging lower CLR (OR 0.35, p<0.001), favoring daily volumetric IGRT. Technique, not just patient selection, moved local control.
studies.monday_clinic: In cT2-T4aN0M0 MIBC pts already committed to bladder-preserving TMT, this favors daily volumetric IGRT and 5-FU-based chemoRT for local control; it does not inform the upstream TMT-versus-radical-cystectomy choice or node-positive/metastatic disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Multicenter **retrospective** cohort, Spain 2010-2022, **N=369** treated with definitive TMT (maximal TURBT + concurrent chemoRT). Multivariable logistic regression for predictors of complete local response.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **cT2-T4aN0M0** muscle-invasive bladder. Median age **76**, **85.1% male**. Node-positive and metastatic disease excluded by staging.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **Image-guidance quality** tracked with local control: weekly portal imaging predicted **lower CLR (OR 0.35, p<0.001)**, favoring daily volumetric IGRT; **VMAT** showed a non-significant favorable trend. RT dose/fractionation not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy. **5-FU-based CRT** predicted **higher CLR (OR 4.9, 95% CI 1.1-22.1, p=0.038)**.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** complete local response (CLR). Secondary: OS, CSS, recurrence patterns, salvage cystectomy.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: CLR **63.7%**. Disease progression **28.8%** (local 10.1%, systemic 10.7%, combined 8.7%). Salvage cystectomy **9.7%**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents cT2-T4aN0M0 MIBC selected for definitive bladder-preserving TMT at European centers. Does not represent node-positive or metastatic disease, or pts triaged to upfront radical cystectomy.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Retrospective, non-randomised; CLR predictors **exploratory** and confounded by treatment era. No head-to-head vs radical cystectomy; RT technique detail and CLR-survival effect sizes absent from source.

open_questions: Optimal concurrent chemoradiotherapy regimen for bladder preservation
Whether daily volumetric IGRT improves local control vs weekly imaging
Randomised TMT vs radical cystectomy comparison

related_trials.brief_title: Treating Muscle-invasive Bladder Cancer With A Non-surgical Method Consisting of Anti-PD-1 Therapy and Chemoradiation
related_trials.brief_summary: The goal of this Phase 2 trial is to evaluate a non-surgical bladder-preserving treatment mode which consists of neoadjuvant chemotherapy plus anti-programmed cell death protein 1 (anti-PD-1) therapy followed by radiotherapy plus concurrent anti-PD-1 therapy. The main questions it aims to answer are: (i) whether the anti-PD-1 antibody, toripalimab, is effective in treating muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC), when combined with chemoradiation; (ii) whether toripalimab is safe in combination…
related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Pathologically diagnosed bladder malignant tumor via biopsy
* Urothelial carcinoma as the primary histological component
* Pretreatment clinical TNM stage as T2-4aN0M0 or T1-4aN1-2M0 (UICC TNM staging classification, version 8)
* Age between 18 and 75 years old
* Karnofsky…

related_trials.brief_title: Enfortumab Vedotin in Combination With Pembrolizumab vs. Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy (cCRT) in People With Muscle Invasive Bladder Cancer (EV-309)
related_trials.brief_summary: This study is being done to see how well two drugs (enfortumab vedotin and pembrolizumab) work together as a bladder preservation approach to treat patients with muscle invasive bladder cancer. The study will compare these drugs to concurrent chemoradiotherapy that is usually used to treat this cancer (standard of care). The study will enroll patients with muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC) who have cancer that has not spread outside the bladder.
related_trials.eligibility_brief:

* Has histologically confirmed initial diagnosis of muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC) with predominant urothelial histology staged cT2-T4aN0M0
* Tissue comprising muscle-invasive urothelial cancer must be submitted for clinical staging at baseline
* Eligible for and agree…

related_trials.brief_title: Study of Tumour Focused Radiotherapy for Bladder Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: Bladder cancer is the seventh most common cancer in the UK, with 10,399 new cases diagnosed in 2011. In a quarter of these cases the cancer has infiltrated the muscular wall of the bladder (muscle invasive) and is life threatening. This type of bladder cancer is usually treated either with surgical removal of the bladder, or daily radiotherapy treatment (high strength xrays which kill cells), given every day for 4 or 7 weeks. RAIDER will investigate methods which have the potential to improve…
related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Written informed consent
* Age ≥16 years
* Histologically or cytologically confirmed transitional cell carcinoma (TCC) of the bladder
* Unifocal bladder TCC staged T2-T4a N0 M0\*
* Fit to receive a radical course of radiotherapy
* WHO performance status 0-2
* Willing and able…

related_trials.brief_summary: Trimodal therapy (TMT) consisting of transurethral resection of bladder tumors followed by radiotherapy and chemotherapy is a therapeutic alternative in patients with Muscle-Infiltrating Bladder Cancer who are inoperable or refuse surgery. One of the main challenges of TMT is the planning and delivery of radiation therapy. Indeed, the bladder is a mobile hollow organ subject to repletion, with variations in size and shape during and between radiotherapy sessions. Standard radiotherapy…

sites.intro: Largest pooled single-fraction lung SABR series to date, though retrospective with no head-to-head vs multi-fraction and Gy dose unstated.

studies.name: Single-fraction SABR for primary NSCLC and lung oligomets (pooled, n=1687)
studies.tldr: 2-yr local control 90-93% and G3+ AEs 2-3% across 1687 pts (1200 primary NSCLC, 487 oligomets) treated with single-fraction SABR.
details: 🔍 Pooled retrospective analysis, 3 institutions (Peter Mac, Cleveland Clinic, Roswell Park); N=1687 (1200 primary NSCLC, 487 pulmonary oligomets)
🔍 Single-fraction SABR; Gy dose/fractionation not reported in source
📊 Isolated local/locoregional failure very uncommon (authors' summary)
📊 Median PFS 30 mo (primary NSCLC) vs 11 mo (pulmonary oligomets)
⚠️ Retrospective pooled cohort, no randomised or matched comparator vs multi-fraction SABR
⚠️ AE data from 2 of 3 sites only (n=789, no Roswell Park); toxicity denominator ≠ full cohort
⚠️ Single-fraction Gy dose unstated in source, so the specific SF-SABR regimen behind these outcomes is unknown
🔗 Consistent with SAFRON II (TROG 13.01) and RTOG 0915 backing single-fraction lung SABR (both randomised)

figures.caption: LC 90-93% at 2y; G3+ AEs 2-3%; N=1687 (1200 NSCLC, 487 oligomets)

verdict.rationale: Pooled retrospective single-arm cohort, no randomised or matched comparator vs multi-fraction SABR; single-fraction Gy dose unstated. Consistent with SAFRON II / RTOG 0915.
verdict.audience: Primary NSCLC or pulmonary oligometastases, SABR candidates

studies.significance: The transfer gate is the fraction size the source omits: outcomes are labeled single-fraction but no Gy dose is given, so you cannot map this 2y LC of 90-93% onto a specific regimen before offering one visit instead of a multi-fraction course. Toxicity (G3+ 2-3%) comes from 2 of 3 sites only. Moves the single- vs multi-fraction choice for lung SABR.
studies.monday_clinic: In pts with primary NSCLC or a pulmonary oligometastasis being considered for SABR, this supports single-fraction delivery as a lower-visit option with high local control; it does not establish it over a multi-fraction schedule, which this cohort did not compare against.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Pooled **retrospective** analysis of 3 institutions (Peter MacCallum, Cleveland Clinic, Roswell Park). **N=1687** (1200 primary NSCLC, 487 pulmonary oligometastases). No randomisation, no comparator arm; survival curves out to 60 months.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Primary NSCLC (n=1200) and pulmonary oligometastases (n=487) treated with single-fraction SABR. Stage, tumour location, and specific eligibility criteria **not reported in source**.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **Single-fraction SABR** across all pts. The **Gy dose is not reported in source** (single fraction only), so the specific regimen behind these outcomes is unknown, gating whether the result transfers to a given practice.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: No prespecified primary endpoint stated. Reported: local control, overall survival, PFS, and adverse events.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **2y local control 90-93%**; isolated local/locoregional failure very uncommon. Median PFS **30 mo** (NSCLC) vs **11 mo** (oligomets). OS by cohort shown in the figure.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: **G3+ AEs 2-3%** (23/789, 2.9% in the assessed NSCLC subset); G2+ 124/789 (15.7%), any AE 215/789 (27%). AE data from **2 of 3 sites only** (no Roswell Park).

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Consistent with **SAFRON II** (TROG 13.01) and **RTOG 0915** supporting single-fraction lung SABR, but those were randomised; this pooled cohort carries **no comparator**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents pts with primary NSCLC or a pulmonary oligometastasis treated with single-fraction SABR at three academic centres. Does not represent pts evaluated against a multi-fraction SABR comparator, which this cohort did not include.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Retrospective**, no randomised/matched comparator. Single-fraction **Gy dose unstated**. AE denominator excludes one of three sites (n=789 of 1687).

open_questions: Single-fraction vs multi-fraction SABR in a randomised comparison
Optimal single-fraction dose across primary NSCLC and pulmonary oligometastases

related_trials.brief_title: A Pilot Study of Single Fraction Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT) in Central Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: The purpose of this study is to determine if a single treatment of targeted radiation (called SBRT or Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy) can be as safe and effective as multiple doses of radiation in patients with central non-small cell lung cancer

* Prior systemic therapy allowed but no systemic therapy 4-6 weeks prior to stereotactic body…

related_trials.relevance_phrase: single-fx vs multi-fx SBRT in central NSCLC

sites.intro: AUA staging round-up positioning a negative PSMA-PET as the gate to omit ePLND, with post-op pelvic nodal RT as the lower-lymphedema alternative.

studies.name: AUA 2026: ePLND / PSMA PET staging round-up
studies.tldr: PSMA PET NPV ~96% may safely omit ePLND in intermediate-risk pts; 47.7% of nodal mets fall outside the ePLND template.
details: 📊 PSMA PET/CT NPV ~96% for nodal disease; a negative scan may spare PLND
🩻 Intermediate-risk: omit PLND if PSMA PET negative; missed nodes small, likely missed by ePLND too
🔍 Yaxley BJUI 2019 (n=1253): 47.7% of nodal mets fell OUTSIDE the ePLND template

details.text: 📊 Lymphedema by nodal treatment (systematic review, Clinckaert 2022)

details: ⚠️ Tyritzis J Urol 2015 (3544 RP, 547 PLND): 6-10x higher DVT/PE risk with lymphadenectomy
🩻 High-risk: decision individualized; ePLND plus adjuvant/salvage pelvic RT may increase side effects
🔗 PSMA-PET staging can prompt post-op pelvic nodal RT instead of ePLND, limiting lymphedema
⚠️ Roberts PCAN 2024 SWOT: no level 1 evidence for oncologic benefit of ePLND; earlier ARSI use lowers pN relevance

figures.caption: n=1253 primary Ga-PSMA PET/CT staging; 47.7% of nodal metastases outside the ePLND template.

studies.significance: 47.7% of positive nodes fall outside the ePLND template (Yaxley, n=1253), so a negative PSMA PET (NPV ~96%) can route pts to nodal RT rather than dissection. Lymphedema is 0-9% with pelvic LN RT alone vs 19-29% when PLND precedes salvage RT, arguing for one nodal treatment rather than stacking both.
studies.monday_clinic: In intermediate-risk pts with a negative PSMA PET, this supports omitting ePLND and, where nodal treatment is needed, favoring PSMA-guided pelvic RT over dissection-plus-salvage-RT; high-risk staging stays individualized.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Conference round-up synthesizing primary-staging literature: **Yaxley BJUI 2019** (n=1253 Ga-PSMA PET/CT), the **Clinckaert 2022** lymphedema systematic review, **Tyritzis J Urol 2015** (3544 RP), and the **Roberts PCAN 2024** SWOT perspective on ePLND.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **PSMA PET NPV ~96%** for nodal disease; **47.7%** of nodal metastases lie outside the ePLND template. Supports omitting PLND in intermediate-risk pts with a negative scan; GG3 leans on nomograms.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: A negative PSMA PET can route pts to post-op pelvic nodal RT instead of ePLND, limiting lymphedema. The **47.7%** out-of-template nodal rate argues for extended nodal coverage when elective pelvic RT is chosen.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Beyond lymphedema, lymphadenectomy raises DVT/PE risk **6-10x** (Tyritzis). Morbidity peaks when ePLND is stacked with salvage pelvic RT, the main argument against routine dissection.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: **No level 1 evidence** for an oncologic benefit of ePLND. Earlier ARSI use makes pN status less decision-relevant. High-risk staging remains individualized, weighing added morbidity of ePLND-plus-salvage-RT.

open_questions: Does ePLND add oncologic benefit over PSMA-PET staging?
Optimal PSMA-PET nomogram cut-off for omitting PLND

related_trials.brief_title: Efficacy and Safety of Radical Prostatectomy (RP) With or Without Salvage Radiotherapy Versus RP With Extended Pelvic Lymph Node Dissection for Localized Intermediate- and High-risk Prostate Cancer With a Briganti Nomogram≥7%
related_trials.brief_summary:
The goal of this clinical trial is to evaluate efficacy and safety of radical prostatectomy (RP) with or without salvage radiotherapy versus RP with extended pelvic lymph node dissection (ePLND) in males who have localized intermediate/high-risk prostate cancer with a Briganti nomogram no less than 7%. The main questions this study aim to answer are:

1. Is RP with or without salvage radiotherapy non-inferior to RP with ePLND in efficacy for patients with localized intermediate/high-risk…

related_trials.brief_title: Role of Lymphnode Dissection in Men With Prostate Cancer Treated With Radical Prostatectomy
related_trials.brief_summary:
Currently, lymph node dissection is standard of care during prostatectomy of intermediate risk tumors at the Martini Clinic. It allows the assessment of possible but unlikely lymph nodes metastases. In case of lymph node metastasis, depending on the number of affected lymph nodes, an adjuvant radiation with or without additional hormone therapy may be discussed in order to stop or delay further progression of the disease.

Since the procedure carries additional risks, it is controversial. The…

related_trials.brief_title: High Precision Imaging of Prostate Specific Membrane Antigen for Personalized Treatment in Prostate Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: This is an Investigator Initiated, non-commercial, single center, non-randomized, single arm, open label pilot study on 194 patients. The patients are affected by prostate cancer but in two different clinical settings. The first group is characterized by patients who are going to be operated of radical prostatectomy and they will undergo the study imaging preoperatively. The second group experiences biochemical relapse after primary treatment.
related_trials.eligibility_brief:

related_trials.brief_title: Preoperative PSMA PET/CT As Triage for EPLND in Patients Scheduled for RALP (PrePSMA)
related_trials.brief_summary: Extended pelvic lymph node dissection (ePLND) is considered the gold standard for nodal staging in men with prostate cancer (PCa). The aim of this project is to determine if preoperative prostate specific membrane antigen (PSMA) positron emission tomography (PET)/computed tomograpy (CT) can safely replace ePLND as a staging method in PCa patients undergoing robot-assisted laparoscopic radical prostatectomy (RALP).

AUA-2026

PERSPECTIVE

1f) Chock for updates
Clinical
Is extended pelvic lymph node dissection REALLY required for
staging of prostate cancer in the PSMA-PET era?
Matthew J. Roberts 123, John W. Yaxley 134, Johan Stranne, Inge M. van Oort and Derya Tilki C89.10
Strengths
•Confirm metastases not detected by imaging (false-negatives)
• Accurate histopathological staging (false-positives)
• Total tumour burden reduction via excision (if nodal metastases
present)
Opportunities
•Incorporation with PSMA-PET imaging for targeted (Radioguided/
Sentinel) LND
• Improved patient selection considering prevalence of metastatic
disease (higher nomogram cut-off)
Weaknesses
• Limitation of anatomical template (missed metastases outside
template)
• Limitation of histopathology (may not detect all metastatic cells)
•Associated morbidity/complications, impact on health and quality of
life
• Increased intra-operative time
Threats
• Absent Level 1 evidence for significant oncological benefit
• Increasing demand on health services to limit low-value care/
interventions
• Surgeon proficiency/training
• Earlier use of androgen receptor signalling inhibitors and other
medications (pN status less relevant)
Alternative care pathways instead of routine eLND
PSMA-PET for staging may prompt a plan for post-operative pelvic lymph node radiotherapy,
limiting morbidity such as lymphedema, especially in the absence of a prior eND.
PCAN, 2024

Summary/Future Directions
• Low incidence of clinically significant AEs
(G3 + in 2-3%
• Local Control estimates 90-93% at 2 years
• Primary NSCLC: Median PFS 30 months and
median OS 3.5 years.
• Pulmonary Oligometastases: Median PHS 11
months and OS >4 years
• Isolated Local/Locoregional failure very
uncommon
